coredata.dc:description: Polymer-based nanocomposite coatings that are enhanced with nanoparticles have gained recognition as effective materials for antibacterial purposes, providing improved durability and biocidal effectiveness. This research introduces an innovative chitosan-based polymer nanocomposite, enhanced with titanium oxide nanopowders and carbon quantum dots. The material was synthesized via the sol-gel process and applied to 316L stainless steel through dip-coating. Structural and morphological properties, including crystal structure, microstructure, elemental dispersion, particle size distribution, chemical composition, and surface morphology, were thoroughly characterized. The results demonstrated that carbon quantum dots and titanium oxide nanopowders were uniformly dispersed within the chitosan matrix, forming a homogeneous, non-agglomerated coating. The antibacterial efficacy of the synthesized samples against 7 different Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria was assessed through disk diffusion, minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) tests. The results confirmed the antibacterial activity of synthesized samples against most of the bacterial pathogens tested but exhibited stronger antibacterial effects on Gram-negative bacteria compared to Gram-positive bacteria. The largest inhibition zones, measuring 21 mm and 16 mm, were observed for Pseudomonas and E. coli for titanium dioxide nanoparticles and the final nanocomposite, respectively. Additionally, the MIC and MBC values for all 7 bacteria were determined.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer-based nanocomposite coatings that are enhanced with nanoparticles have gained recognition as effective materials for antibacterial purposes, providing improved durability and biocidal effectiveness. This research introduces an innovative chitosan-based polymer nanocomposite, enhanced with titanium oxide nanopowders and carbon quantum dots. The material was synthesized via the sol-gel process and applied to 316L stainless steel through dip-coating. Structural and morphological properties, including crystal structure, microstructure, elemental dispersion, particle size distribution, chemical composition, and surface morphology, were thoroughly characterized. The results demonstrated that carbon quantum dots and titanium oxide nanopowders were uniformly dispersed within the chitosan matrix, forming a homogeneous, non-agglomerated coating. The antibacterial efficacy of the synthesized samples against 7 different Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria was assessed through disk diffusion, minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) tests. The results confirmed the antibacterial activity of synthesized samples against most of the bacterial pathogens tested but exhibited stronger antibacterial effects on Gram-negative bacteria compared to Gram-positive bacteria. The largest inhibition zones, measuring 21 mm and 16 mm, were observed for Pseudomonas and E. coli for titanium dioxide nanoparticles and the final nanocomposite, respectively. Additionally, the MIC and MBC values for all 7 bacteria were determined.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing prevalence of bacterial resistance to antibiotics has heightened the need for alternative antibacterial methods, especially within the healthcare, food packaging, and water purification industries. Nanocomposite coatings based on polymers, enhanced with nanoparticles, have surfaced as a viable solution for developing enduring antibacterial surfaces. These materials integrate the mechanical strength and barrier characteristics of polymers with the antimicrobial efficacy of nanoparticles, providing distinct benefits regarding durability, functionality, and ease of application [1]. Polymer-based nanocomposite coatings have demonstrated significant promise across multiple domains, especially in healthcare environments where bacterial contamination remains a critical concern. In the food sector, these coatings are effective in preventing microbial contamination and prolonging shelf life, whereas in water treatment applications, they can effectively inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilms [2]. Looking ahead, ongoing developments in nanotechnology and polymer science are expected to further improve the efficacy of these materials.

In recent years, there has been significant research into metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, including silver (Ag), copper (Cu), manganese oxide (MnO2), magnesium oxide (MgO), zinc oxide (ZnO), and titanium dioxide (TiO2), due to their antibacterial characteristics. When these nanoparticles are incorporated into a polymer matrix, they have the ability to release ions that disrupt bacterial cell walls, interfere with intracellular functions, and ultimately lead to cell death [3–6]. Nanoparticles exhibit a significant surface area-to-volume ratio, facilitating enhanced interaction with bacterial cells. This characteristic enhances their antibacterial properties, even at low concentrations, thereby reducing the risk of potential toxicity [7]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanoparticles (NPs) have garnered significant attention in research for their antibacterial characteristics, attributed to their capacity to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) when exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light. This generation of ROS can compromise bacterial cell walls, ultimately resulting in cell death [8]. The photocatalytic activity is attributed to the excitation of electrons in TiO2 when exposed to ultraviolet light, leading to the generation of hydroxyl radicals and superoxide ions. These reactive species subsequently target critical bacterial components, including lipids, proteins, and DNA [9]. In addition to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) also attach to bacterial cell membranes, causing membrane stress and thereby amplifying their bactericidal effects [10,11]. Due to their potent oxidative characteristics and low toxicity, TiO2 NPs remain a highly promising option for the development of antimicrobial coatings and surface treatments.

Polymeric matrices serve as versatile scaffolds for the distribution and stabilization of nanoparticles, which is essential for ensuring uniform antibacterial efficacy. Additionally, the functionalization of nanoparticles with particular types of polymers can enhance the compatibility between the nanoparticles and the polymer matrix, thus improving the dispersion of nanoparticles and the longevity of the coating [12]. Numerous polymers, such as polyethylene (PE), polypropylene (PP), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and polyvinyl chloride (PVC), have been utilized in the creation of nanocomposite coatings [13,14]. Biocompatible polymers, including chitosan and polylactic acid (PLA), are favored for biomedical and food-related applications due to their environmentally friendly characteristics and safety [15]. Chitosan, a natural polysaccharide obtained from chitin, is well-known for its significant antibacterial characteristics, rendering it an important biopolymer for use in antimicrobial applications. The antibacterial efficacy of chitosan is mainly due to its positively charged amino groups, which interact with the negatively charged membranes of bacterial cells. This interaction results in enhanced cell permeability, the leakage of intracellular substances, and ultimately, the death of the bacterial cells [16]. Chitosan also possesses the ability to impede DNA replication in bacteria by penetrating the cell and attaching to DNA, which subsequently diminishes bacterial viability [17–19]. Its biocompatibility, biodegradability, and non-toxic characteristics render chitosan an excellent candidate for the creation of antibacterial coatings, packaging solutions, and various biomedical applications [19].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a category of carbon-derived nanomaterials characterized by distinctive optical and electronic attributes, such as photoluminescence and biocompatibility. These features render them highly suitable for antibacterial applications. CQDs can impede bacterial proliferation through various mechanisms, including the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and direct engagement with bacterial membranes, which results in membrane disruption and oxidative stress within bacterial cells [20]. CQDs can be modified to improve their interaction with bacterial cells, thereby increasing their effectiveness against various bacteria, encompassing both Gram-positive and Gram-negative strains [21]. Furthermore, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are regarded as a safer and more environmentally sustainable alternative to conventional metal-based nanoparticles, as they do not emit toxic ions that could potentially jeopardize human health or ecological systems. Research is ongoing to investigate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a potential antibacterial agent. Recent studies have concentrated on optimizing their surface characteristics and enhancing the efficiency of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation to achieve improved bactericidal effects [22].

Stainless steel 316L, a low-carbon form of stainless steel 316, is recognized for its outstanding resistance to corrosion, strength, and biocompatibility, rendering it particularly appropriate for medical-grade uses in healthcare settings. The reduced carbon content minimizes the likelihood of carbide precipitation during the welding process, thereby improving its resistance to pitting and crevice corrosion, particularly in environments rich in chlorides, such as bodily fluids [1,23]. The characteristics of these materials guarantee their durability and dependability in the manufacture of various medical devices, such as surgical instruments, orthopedic implants, and cardiovascular devices, where exceptional corrosion resistance and strength are critical [24]. 316L stainless steel also possesses significant tensile strength and is capable of enduring multiple sterilization cycles, which is essential for upholding hygiene standards and minimizing infection risks in medical environments [25]. The material's biocompatibility guarantees a reduced likelihood of negative reactions when in direct contact with human tissues, thereby reinforcing its extensive application in medical implants and surgical instruments.

Numerous challenges persist in the advancement of effective and safe polymer-nanoparticle coatings, despite their potential. It is essential to attain a uniform distribution of nanoparticles within the polymer matrix to ensure reliable antibacterial efficacy. Furthermore, investigations have concentrated on mechanisms for controlled release to sustain long-lasting antibacterial effects, alongside the creation of biocompatible coatings suitable for delicate applications [26].

This study focused on fabricating a polymeric nanobiocomposite coating with a unique composition and was applied on 316L medical-grade stainless steel using the sol-gel dip-coating method. The structural characteristics and antibacterial properties of the nanobiocomposite were thoroughly analyzed and tested. The novelty of this work, compared to previous works in the literature, lies in its integration of a biocompatible polymer (chitosan), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2) into a cohesive nanobiocomposite, applied specifically to 316L stainless steel using the sol-gel dip-coating method. This combination of materials and methods offers a durable, scalable, and highly effective antibacterial solution tailored for medical applications. This study distinguishes itself from earlier research, which typically concentrated on CQD/TiO2 in isolation or in less complex formulations, by incorporating chitosan, a biopolymer known for its inherent antibacterial characteristics, together with CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles. The interaction among these elements improves the effectiveness and biocompatibility of the coating, an area that has not been thoroughly investigated in previous studies. This research distinguishes itself as a notable progression in the creation of antibacterial surfaces for medical devices and equipment by tackling practical issues, including the uniform distribution of nanoparticles, the durability of the coating, and biocompatibility.

Titanium ethoxide [(Ti(OC2H5)4), %95], ethanol (C2H5OH, %100) and nitric acid (HNO3, %65) were purchased from Merck. Chitosan with medium molecular weight (190 kDa), ethylenediamine [(NH2CH2CH2NH2), ≥%99] and acetic acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Medium MW chitosan was selected due to its optimal blend of processability, mechanical strength, antibacterial characteristics, nanoparticle stabilization, and biocompatibility. These attributes are crucial for the effective synthesis and functionality of the nanocomposite coating [27,28]. Mueller Hinton Broth and Mueller Hinton Agar were obtained from QUELAB. Distilled water was used throughout all experiments.

Moghadam et al. employed a synthesis method to produce carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [29,30]. The procedure commenced with the dissolution of 1.24 g of citric acid and 1.5 g of ethylenediamine in 35 ml of deionized water. This mixture was subsequently placed in an autoclave and subjected to heating at 180 °C for a duration of 6 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the reactor was permitted to cool to room temperature naturally. The resultant product, which was a transparent brownish-yellow solution, was isolated through centrifugation and underwent further purification via dialysis. Ultimately, a dark brown solid was acquired through vacuum distillation, followed by a drying process.

Titania nanoparticles were produced through the sol-gel technique. Initially, 1.75 cc of Titanium ethoxide was dissolved in 5 cc of ethanol, and this mixture was stirred at 400 rpm for 15 min, referred to as Solution A. Subsequently, 0.75 cc of water and 0.6 cc of nitric acid were dissolved in ethanol and stirred at the same speed for 15 min, designated as Solution B. Solution B was then added gradually to Solution A while maintaining vigorous stirring, and the combined solution was refluxed at 80 °C for 2 h with stirring at 1200 rpm. This process resulted in the formation of a blue titania sol. The titania sol was subsequently heated in an oven at 80 °C for 24 h to yield a gel. Finally, the dried gel underwent calcination at 400 °C.

Initially, individual solutions of chitosan, titanium oxide nanoparticles, and carbon quantum dots were prepared. Specifically, 0.03 g of the biopolymer chitosan was dissolved in 15 ml of 1 % acetic acid, while 1.0 g of titanium dioxide nanoparticle powder was mixed with 30 ml of 1 % acetic acid, and 0.4 g of carbon quantum dots was dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water, each in separate beakers. Each solution underwent stirring for 60 min at a speed of 900 rpm using a magnetic stirrer to achieve optimal dispersion and mixing. Following this, the titanium dioxide nanoparticle solution was gradually added to the chitosan solution while maintaining stirring. Subsequently, the carbon quantum dots solution was introduced dropwise (at a rate of one drop every 2 s) into the combined solution. The resulting mixture was then stirred on a magnetic stirrer for an additional 90 min at a speed of 900 rpm. To ensure thorough homogenization, the beaker containing the nanocomposite solution was subjected to ultrasound treatment for 15 min, followed by drying in an oven at 85 °C for 48 h. Finally, the dried composite sample was milled for a duration of 15 min.

To prepare for the coating application, it is essential to ensure that the substrate is thoroughly cleaned of any dust, grease, rust, paint, chemicals, and other contaminants. L316 steel components measuring 20 × 20 × 2 mm were initially cut to size, followed by sanding and polishing using a metallographic grinding and polishing machine equipped with sandpapers ranging from 80 to 3000 grit. The sample was subsequently polished with industrial soapy water to achieve a smooth, mirror-like finish. Following this, the substrate underwent a cleaning process for 15 min in an ultrasonic bath filled with ethanol, followed by another cleaning in an ultrasonic bath containing acetone. Finally, the substrates were rinsed with distilled water and dried in an oven at a temperature of 50 °C for a duration of 2 h.

According to the solubilization process described in the previous section, composite solutions with varying concentrations of components were prepared for coating, as detailed in Table 1 . In this table, the amount of carbon quantum dots was kept constant, and the concentration variations of other components, chitosan and TiO2 nanoparticles, were investigated to achieve maximum synergy in antibacterial activity as well as uniform distribution of nanoparticles within the polymeric matrix. The authors in their previous work [29], using the Taguchi design, showed that the maximum antibacterial activity observed at 0.4 g of CQDs. To ensure a uniform distribution and dispersion of the components, the solutions were subjected to stirring with a magnetic stirrer for 90 min at a high speed of 900 rpm, followed by an additional 15 min in an ultrasonic bath. A dip coater was then employed to immerse the substrate in the solution at a consistent speed of 4 cm/min for a duration of 1 min. Subsequently, the substrate was dried vertically in an oven at 80 °C for 10 h. This procedure was repeated six times. The results of the coatings, derived from solutions with varying concentrations, demonstrated that the solution with a higher concentration of chitosan yielded more suitable and uniform coatings due to its increased viscosity.

The composition and structure of the synthesized nanocomposites and their components were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). This analysis was conducted with the AVATAR model from Thermo Company, spanning a spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1. To investigate the crystallographic properties, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Philips pw1730 model, covering a 2Ө range of 10° to 80°. A copper cathode was employed during the analysis, producing a wavelength of 1.54056 Å, with a current of 30 amps and a voltage of 40 kV. The microstructural analysis of carbon quantum dots and TiO2 nanoparticles was carried out using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips CM120) at an applied voltage of 120 kV. To further examine the elemental composition, X-ray surface elemental mapping was performed by SAMX Company using EDS and X-ray Map techniques, providing detailed maps of element distribution and dispersion within the nanocomposite and its coating. Additionally, the EDS (Broker model) X-ray energy diffraction spectroscopy was employed to generate a size histogram and analyze the constituent elements and their quantities in the samples. The morphology and size distribution of the nanocomposite and coating were examined using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, TESCAN MIRA3) operating at 30 kV. The thickness of the coating was determined through cross-sectional field emission scanning electron microscopy. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the nanocomposite was measured using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Thermo Company) across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm.

An adhesion test was conducted to assess the strength of the bionanocomposite coating in accordance with the ASTM D 4541 standard. Aluminum dollies were affixed to the chitosan and bionanocomposite-coated substrate. The adhesive was allowed to cure completely by maintaining the sample at a temperature of 25 °C for a duration of 48 h. A slot was created around the dollies, and the dollies were pulled perpendicularly to the coating surface at a rate of 10 mm/min until the coating detached from the substrate.

The antibacterial assessment of the nanocomposite and its constituent materials was performed to determine their efficacy against various Gram-negative bacteria, such as Shigella, E. coli, Klebsiella, and Pseudomonas, in addition to Gram-positive bacteria, including Enterococcus, Staphylococcus, and Streptococcus. All bacterial strains were sourced from the Iranian Biological Resource Center. The evaluation methods employed comprised the agar well diffusion assay, the determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), and the assessment of the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BHI agar, Mueller-Hinton broth and Mueller-Hinton agar mediums
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare BHI agar medium, 4.10 g of BHI agar powder is dissolved in 200 ml of high-purity distilled water in an Erlenmeyer flask and stirred for 15 min until homogeneous. The solution is then sealed and sterilized in an autoclave at 123 °C for 20 min under 15 psi pressure. After removal and cooling, the solution is poured into petri dishes at room temperature to form a thin layer on the surface. The petri dishes are then stored in a refrigerator at a temperature of 2–8 °C.

To prepare Mueller-Hinton broth medium, 10.5 g of Mueller-Hinton broth powder is measured and dissolved in high-purity distilled water to a final volume of 500 ml. The solution is stirred for 30 min and then sterilized in an autoclave at 123 °C and 15 psi pressure for 20 min. After cooling to 45 °C, the medium is distributed into screw-capped tubes that were previously sterilized in the autoclave. The tubes are then stored in a refrigerator at a temperature of 2–8 °C.

To prepare 500 ml of solid Mueller-Hinton agar medium, 19 g of Mueller-Hinton agar powder is added to an Erlenmeyer flask containing 500 ml of 99 % pure distilled water. The mixture is stirred at 85 °C and 90 rpm until fully dissolved. The resulting solution is sterilized in an autoclave at 123 °C and 15 psi pressure for 20 min. After cooling in open air to 45 °C, the medium is poured into sterilized petri dishes. The plates are then stored in a refrigerator at a temperature of 2–8 °C.

To assess the antibacterial properties of the nanocomposite and its constituents, an agar well diffusion assay was performed on seven bacterial pathogens previously identified. Initially, a small quantity of each bacterial colony, sourced from the BHI agar medium, was transferred with a sterile loop into test tubes containing Muller-Hinton broth, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 24 h. The turbidity of the bacterial culture was compared to that of the standard McFarland medium to ensure appropriate bacterial growth (To prepare the 0.5 McFarland standard, 0.5 ml of barium chloride (BaCl) at a concentration of 48.0 M is combined with 5.99 ml of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) at a concentration of 18.0 M). Subsequently, a sterile swab was dipped into the bacterial culture and spread across the surface of a Muller-Hinton agar plate to establish a lawn culture. The plates were then sealed in the laboratory for 10 min to facilitate bacterial growth and stabilization on the agar surface. Wells were formed in the agar using the end of a sterile micropipette tip. A 50 mg quantity of the nanocomposite and its components was dissolved in 1 ml of 5 % acetic acid, followed by 30 min of homogenization using a magnetic stirrer and ultrasound. Following this, 90 μL of the prepared solution was introduced into the wells on the agar plate using a micropipette. A tetracycline antibiotic disk served as a positive control, while a blank disk (lacking any drug) acted as a negative control. The bacterial plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, after which the diameters of the zones of growth inhibition were measured.

The outcomes of the disk diffusion assay were presented as means ± standard deviation (SD) and analyzed utilizing SPSS software. A Student's t-test was employed to compare the results of the disk diffusion assay, with P values <0.01 (P < 0.01) deemed significant.

To conduct the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) test, 50 mg of nanocomposite powder and its individual components were each mixed with 1 ml of 5 % acetic acid. The resulting mixtures were then homogenized for 30 min using a magnetic stirrer, followed by treatment in an ultrasonic bath. Serial dilution was carried out as follows: eight microtubes, each containing 2 ml, were prepared, and 25.0 ml of Muller-Hinton broth was added to each. Subsequently, 25.0 ml of the nanocomposite solutions and their components were introduced into the first microtube, resulting in a total volume of 0.5 ml. An 8-step serial dilution was performed by transferring 25.0 ml from the first microtube to the second, and this process continued until the eighth microtube. At this stage, 0.25 ml was removed from the eighth microtube. This procedure was repeated for all nanocomposite solutions and their components. Following this, 0.1 ml of a bacterial culture medium, which had been prepared by inoculating Muller-Hinton broth with a bacterial stock and incubating for 24 h, was added to each microtube. All microtubes were then incubated at 25 °C for 24 h. After incubation, a loop was taken from each microtube and cultured on sterile Muller-Hinton agar, using two agar plates per bacterium, divided into four sections. The bacterial cultures were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The MIC and MBC values were assessed by comparing the levels of bacterial growth at various dilutions on the plates. The MIC was defined as the lowest concentration that inhibited visible bacterial growth after overnight incubation, while the MBC was identified as the lowest concentration that eliminated 99.9 % of the bacterial growth [31].

ISO standard 10993-5 was employed to assess the cytotoxicity of the nanobiocomposite. The extraction process was conducted in accordance with ISO standard 10993-12 [32]. The NIH/3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblast cell line (ATCC CRL-1658, USA) was selected as the cellular model for this study. The NIH/3T3 cell line is one of the most frequently used lines in material/cell interaction research. Initially, the cells were seeded at a concentration of 1 × 10^5 cells/ml and allowed to pre-incubate in micro-titration test plates (TPP, Switzerland) for 24 h. Subsequently, the cultivation medium was replaced with a pre-prepared mixture of culture media containing the extracts, followed by an additional incubation period of 24 h. Cell viability was then assessed using the MTT assay [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2 nanoparticles and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size distribution of carbon quantum dots and TiO2 nanoparticles were analyzed using tunneling electron microscopy and the results are brought in Fig. 1(a, b, c and d). Fig. 1(a and c) shows the shape of CQDs is spherical, while, TiO2 nanoparticles have an almost spherical shape and both nanoparticles are evenly dispersed. Also, Fig. 1(b and d) exhibit the average size distribution of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots falls within the range of 20–40 nm and 2–10 nm, respectively.

The characterization of the compounds and the determination of the phases of the synthesized nanocomposite and its constituents were performed through X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, with the findings illustrated in Fig. 2 . The X-ray diffraction pattern of TiO2 nanoparticles closely corresponds to the reference pattern for the anatase crystalline phase of titanium dioxide (JCPDS no. 073-1764). The peaks identified at 2θ angles of 25°, 38°, 48°, 53°, 56°, and 63° are associated with the crystal planes (100), (004), (200), (105), (211), and (204), respectively, which are consistent with the diffraction pattern of the anatase phase [34–36]. The absence of peaks related to the rutile phase in this diffraction pattern confirms the presence of a pure anatase phase in the nanoparticles.

The X-ray diffraction analysis of carbon quantum dots indicates the presence of amorphous carbon in addition to hexagonal carbon. A prominent broad peak is detected at 2θ = 18.39°, which aligns with the (103) crystal plane of hexagonal carbon. Furthermore, a less intense peak at 2θ = 26.84° is noted, which is associated with the (002) crystal plane of graphite, thereby affirming the crystallinity of the sample [37].

The diffraction pattern of medium molecular weight chitosan reveals a broad peak at 2θ = 20.16°, which corresponds to the (200) crystal plane. This observation indicates the presence of partial crystalline regions within the amorphous matrix of chitosan. Notably, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern does not exhibit any impurity peaks. Previous studies have identified a weaker peak at 2θ = 10°, linked to the (202) crystal plane; however, this peak is absent in the current sample, likely due to variations in the degree of deacetylation during the chitosan production process. Chitosan is obtained through the deacetylation of marine shells, including those from shrimp, fish, certain crustaceans, insects, and fungi. Generally, samples with lower deacetylation exhibit higher crystallinity, which explains the significantly reduced intensity of the diffraction peak at 2θ = 10° in chitosan derived from fungal sources compared to other origins [38].

The X-ray diffraction patterns of both two and three-component nanocomposites reveal only the peaks corresponding to titania nanoparticles, with no diffraction peaks from the other components present in the composite patterns. In general, multi-component mixtures and nanocomposites tend to exhibit overlapping peaks. Quantitative X-ray diffraction analysis can generally be classified into two categories: the first involves all components being crystalline and identifiable, while the second pertains to scenarios where some components are non-crystalline or are not detectable due to overlapping peaks or quantities that fall below the detection limit of the instrument [39]. In certain instances, the overlap of components leads to minor displacements in the positions of individual peaks. In the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the current nanocomposite, the closeness of the characteristic peaks from the various components, particularly the overlap of the chitosan peak at 2θ = 20.16° and the carbon quantum dot peak at 2θ = 18.39° with the titania nanoparticle peak at 2θ = 25°, results in the visibility of only the titania nanoparticle peaks. Moreover, during the dissolution of chitosan in acetic acid and the subsequent formation of hydrogen bonds, chitosan significantly loses its crystallinity [40,41]. Nevertheless, as will be discussed in subsequent sections, findings from additional analyses unequivocally demonstrate the presence of both the chitosan biopolymer and carbon dots within the nanocomposite and coating. Furthermore, a comparison of the diffraction patterns of the individual components and the nanocomposites reveals that the overlap of the component diffraction patterns diminishes the intensity of the peaks observed in the X-ray diffraction pattern of the nanocomposite.

FTIR analysis was conducted on the three-component and two-component nanocomposites, as well as their individual components, to identify functional groups and bonds between elements, with the results illustrated in Fig. 3 . The spectrum of chitosan exhibited several distinctive peaks. Notably, the absorption peak at 3414 cm−1 is attributed to the overlapping vibrational-stretching peaks of NH and OH groups [42]. The peak at 2878 cm−1 is attributed to the CH stretching bond [43], while the absorption peak at 1616 cm−1 corresponds to CO groups, and the absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 is associated with the NH bending vibration (amide group). The two peaks mentioned are the primary distinguishing features of the chitosan biopolymer. The absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 is associated with methyl bonds (CH3), while the peaks detected at 1155 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 pertain to the vibrations of CH bonds and CO groups, respectively [42,43].

In the spectrum of TiO2 nanoparticles, a prominent absorption peak at 3413 cm−1 indicates the existence of surface hydroxyl groups. The absorption peaks observed at 1636 cm−1 and 1616 cm−1 are likely linked to structural water hydroxyl groups and the bending vibration of OH bonds. Additionally, significant peaks at 617 cm−1 and 476 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of TiOTi bonds, thereby affirming the presence of the TiO2 phase within the material's composition [34,44].

In the infrared spectrum of carbon quantum dots, the prominent absorption peak observed at 3414 cm−1 signifies the existence of surface hydroxyl (OH) and amino (NH2) groups. Additionally, a peak at 3236 cm−1 is linked to the hydrogen bonding of the NH group. The absorption peak at 1707 cm−1 is representative of the stretching vibration of the CO double bond, whereas the peak at 1638 cm−1 pertains to the stretching vibrations of the amide CO and CN double bonds [45]. An absorption peak for the NH group is also observed at 1550 cm−1. Adjacent peaks at 1294 cm−1 and 1229 cm−1 are both attributed to CO bond vibrations [80]. Stretching vibrations of the CO and CC bonds are observed at 1183 cm−1 and 777 cm−1, respectively, which are characteristic vibrations indicating the presence of carbon quantum dots in a composition [46].

In two-component chitosan-TiO2 nanoparticle nanocomposite spectrum, characteristic peaks in the wavelength range of 400–900 cm−1 correspond to TiOTi stretching vibration, the peak at 1094 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching bond and CN bending vibration [47], and the peak at 1388.43 cm−1 relates to methyl group (CH3) bond vibrations. The prominent peak observed at 1627.96 cm−1 is attributed to the NH bending vibration, a distinctive feature of chitosan. Additionally, the peak located at 3416.47 cm−1 represents a combined vibrational-stretching peak associated with the NH vibrations of the NH2 and OH groups present in chitosan. These findings validate the synthesis of the chitosan-TiO2 nanoparticle nanocomposite [48].

The detected peak in the infrared spectrum for the three-component chitosan/TiO2 nanoparticle/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite at 480 cm−1 is associated with the TiOTi bond. The broad absorption peak at 3428 cm−1 is linked to the stretching vibration of surface hydroxyl groups. Furthermore, the identification of the CC double bond at 1556 cm−1 and the ether COC group at 1090 cm−1 may indicate the presence of carbon quantum dots [49]. The absorption peaks at 1633 cm−1 and 1703 cm−1 correspond to vibration NH of the amino group (NH2), which may indicate interaction of Ti4+ with NH2 [47]. All spectra and observed peaks confirm the presence of the three-component nanocomposite elements and the formation of the final nanocomposite.

The UV–Vis spectroscopy results for the nanocomposite and its constituent materials within the wavelength range of 200–1000 nm are presented in Fig. 4 . This figure illustrates that TiO2 nanoparticles exhibit peak absorption at a wavelength of 310 nm. Prior research has demonstrated that TiO2 nanoparticles possess the capability to decompose adjacent molecules when exposed to UV light, showcasing photocatalytic properties typically within the absorption wavelength range of 300–380 nm. Notably, titania nanoparticles are capable of absorbing the majority of light with wavelengths under 390 nm [50].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) typically exhibit significant absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) range, specifically between 200 and 380 nm, while demonstrating minimal absorption in the visible spectrum. The absorption peak identified at approximately 240 nm is associated with the π-π* transition of carbon-carbon (CC) bonds. Additionally, peaks around 325 nm and 375 nm correspond to the n-π* transitions of carbon-oxygen (CO) and carbon‑nitrogen (CN) bonds, respectively. The gradual extension of absorption into the visible spectrum is attributed to low-energy surface states, which are influenced by surface functional groups. Occasionally, an absorption band may be detected in the visible region, linked to functional groups such as amines. Furthermore, the absorption dip noted at 287 nm is ascribed to the π-π* transition of CN bonds found in aromatic rings, which typically do not contribute to emission. CQDs are characterized by their distinctive ability to facilitate light-induced electron transfer, thereby enhancing the nanocomposite's capacity for ultraviolet light absorption [51–53].

The absorption spectrum of the final nanocomposite reveals that the absorption peaks of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are closely positioned and overlap, resulting in a singular UV absorption peak for the nanocomposite at approximately 300 nm. Furthermore, the UV-protective characteristics of TiO2 contribute to a peak intensity for the nanocomposite that is considerably lower than the individual peaks of the carbon quantum dots and titanium dioxide nanoparticles. Previous research has corroborated these findings, indicating that the UV-protective properties of TiO2 nanoparticles lead to a significantly reduced UV spectrum transmission through the titania-reinforced nanocomposite film compared to that of pure quantum dots [54,55]. Additionally, the results indicate that the chitosan polymer does not absorb in this wavelength range, allowing the majority of wavelengths to pass through, thereby failing to offer any protection against UV radiation [30].

FESEM images along with the results from energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) for the two-component (Chitosan/TiO2 NPs) and three-component (Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs) nanocomposites are presented in Fig. 5 . The nanoparticles are observed as spherical, aggregated entities distributed over the surface of the nanocomposite, as illustrated in Fig. 4a and c. This aggregation may result from the high specific surface area of the titania nanoparticles and CQDs, coupled with their strong propensity to agglomerate within the chitosan polymer matrix, which can lead to undesirable cold sintering of the nanoparticles.

The EDS analysis results for the Chitosan/TiO2 NPs nanocomposite indicate the presence of carbon (25.17 wt%), oxygen (34 wt%), titanium (27.45 wt%), and nitrogen (13.38 wt%) as illustrated in Fig. 4b. In contrast, the Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite reveals the detection of carbon (13.36 wt%), oxygen (27.36 wt%), titanium (24.98 wt%), and nitrogen (2.62 wt%) as shown in Fig. 4d. These findings unequivocally demonstrate the successful dispersion of TiO2 NPs and CQDs within the chitosan matrix, leading to the formation of the nanocomposites.

Fig. 6a illustrates the morphology and microstructure of the Stainless Steel 316L substrate. Additionally, Fig. 6(b and c) and (d and e) depict the morphology and microstructure of the Chitosan/TiO2 nanoparticles and the Chitosan/TiO2 nanoparticles/CQDs nanocomposite coatings at two distinct magnifications. Research indicates that the surface of pure chitosan is predominantly uniform and smooth [56]. From Fig. 6(c), it is evident that TiO2 nanoparticles are uniformly distributed throughout the chitosan polymer matrix. In the three-component nanocomposite coating depicted in Fig. 6(d) and (e), carbon quantum dots are not discernible due to their muted color and integration within the carbon matrix of the chitosan. The images illustrate that the coating is nearly uniform, with minor cracks likely resulting from the rate of heat treatment and the drying process. Fig. 6(f) presents a backscattered electron image of the final nanocomposite coating, confirming the establishment of a uniform phase of nanoparticles within the polymer matrix. Fig. 6(g) and (h) display the cross-section of the substrate both with and without the coating, respectively. Fig. 7(h) clearly demonstrates the presence of the coating on the substrate. The average thickness measured at various points across the cross-section of the coating is approximately 2.5 μm.

The findings from the linear EDS conducted on the surfaces of Chitosan/TiO2 NPs and Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite coatings at twenty distinct locations are presented in Fig. 7(b and c) and Table 2 . These results clearly indicate that the elements titanium, carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen indicative of the functional groups associated with the carbon quantum dots are dispersed throughout the coating in varying weight percentages. The elemental distribution percentages outlined in Table 2 further validate the nearly uniform presence of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots within the chitosan matrix.

Fig. 8 presents the elemental distribution map for the Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite, along with the Chitosan/TiO2 NPs and Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite coatings. This map highlights the distribution of carbon, titanium, nitrogen (which serves as an indicator of the presence of carbon quantum dots, as indicated in prior research [49]), and oxygen. The results of these tests demonstrate an effective distribution of CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles within both the nanocomposite and coating structures, thereby confirming their successful integration.

The adhesion strength of the substrate with both the neat polymer matrix and the nanocomposite coatings was assessed. The polymer coating, which did not contain nanoparticles, exhibited an adhesion strength of 6.4 MPa, whereas the nanocomposite coating demonstrated a higher adhesion strength of 7.5 MPa. It is evident that the adhesion strength of the neat chitosan coating is inferior to that of the nanocomposite coating. This indicates that the chitosan coating, when enhanced with a dispersion of nanoparticles, shows a greater tendency to adhere to the substrate. The enhancement of adhesion strength of chitosan coating on stainless steel through the incorporation of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots occurs via several mechanisms. TiO2 contributes to an increase in surface roughness, facilitating mechanical interlocking, whereas carbon quantum dots enhance chemical bonding by adding functional groups. The uniform distribution of these nanomaterials also improves the coating's homogeneity and wettability, leading to superior contact with the substrate. Furthermore, the positive charge of chitosan engages in electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged oxide layer present on stainless steel, thereby further reinforcing adhesion [57,58].

The disk diffusion assay was performed on seven bacterial pathogens: E. coli, Staphylococcus, Pseudomonas, Shigella, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, and Streptococcus. This assay evaluated the antibacterial efficacy of titanium dioxide nanoparticles and a Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite, with the results illustrated in Fig. 9 and Table 3 . The results revealed that there was no inhibition zone surrounding the blank disk (negative control), whereas a significant inhibition zone was noted around the antibiotic disk, indicating its effective inhibitory action. The experimental data validated the antibacterial efficacy of titanium dioxide nanoparticles and the resultant nanocomposite against the majority of the bacterial pathogens examined (see Fig. 10 ). The average antibacterial activity of TiO2 NPs, nanocomposite and tetracycline disk against bacterial groups ranged from 15–21, 7–16, 16–20 mm, respectively (zone of inhibition). The most significant inhibition zones recorded were 21 ∓ 0.7 mm for Pseudomonas and 16 ∓ 0.4 mm for E. coli, attributed to titanium dioxide nanoparticles and the final nanocomposite, respectively. These findings are primarily associated with the distinctive antimicrobial properties of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which arise from the presence of active oxygen radicals in the solution. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as hydroxyl and superoxide radicals, frequently result in the degradation of DNA and proteins, ultimately leading to the death of bacterial cells. Chitosan, a cationic biopolymer, demonstrates antibacterial activity through its interaction with the negatively charged cell walls of bacteria. This interaction leads to the disruption of the cell membrane, causing the leakage of intracellular substances and ultimately resulting in the destruction of bacterial cells [11,21]. Moreover, Streptococcus displayed resistance to both the nanoparticles and the nanocomposite, as evidenced by the absence of an inhibition zone for this particular bacterial strain. In summary, the findings suggest that both nanoparticles and nanocomposites possess more potent antibacterial effects against Gram-negative bacteria than against Gram-positive bacteria.

The findings from the MIC and MBC assessments of titanium dioxide nanoparticles and a Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite against bacterial pathogens are illustrated in Fig. 11 and detailed in Table 3. In Table 4 , concentrations of the nanocomposite that exhibit minimal or no antibacterial effect are indicated with an asterisk (*). It is important to note that the disk diffusion test results revealed no activity from Streptococcus in response to either the titanium dioxide nanoparticles or the Chitosan/TiO2 NPs/CQDs nanocomposite; therefore, MIC and MBC evaluations were not performed for this particular bacterium. Gram-positive bacteria are characterized by a robust peptidoglycan layer within their cell wall, which imparts considerable mechanical strength. Metallic ions, such as Ti4+, are attracted to the thiol groups (-SH) present on the proteins situated on the outer surface of the cell membrane. These ions infiltrate the cell wall, leading to the denaturation of proteins and undermining the integrity of the bacterial membrane. Furthermore, the structural properties of the nanocomposite play a role in mechanically disrupting the membrane [59].

The integration of carbon dots into the polymer matrix has significantly improved its mechanical properties and enhanced its ability to generate free radicals. This alteration amplifies the polymer's physical and chemical reactivity, particularly in the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). These ROS can cause oxidative damage by interfering with critical bacterial enzymes, ultimately resulting in cellular disintegration and lysis [60]. Furthermore, the application of chitosan as a coating for metal oxides such as TiO2 serves to reduce their potential adverse effects. This strategy results in a sophisticated inorganic-biopolymer nanocomposite that exhibits increased stability, enhanced antibacterial performance, and a more rapid response compared to unmodified chitosan [61].

The results of MTT assay for investigation of cytotoxicity of the synthesized nanocomposite illustrated in Fig. 12 . The results clearly indicate that the nanocomposites do not exhibit cytotoxic effects, highlighting their potential as a protective coating for medical devices during short-term interactions with the human body.

A novel chitosan polymer nanocomposite, reinforced with TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots, was synthesized in this research and subsequently applied to 316L stainless steel through the sol-gel dip-coating technique. The results exhibited that average size distribution of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots falls within the range of 20–40 nm and 2–10 nm, respectively. The results demonstrated that carbon quantum dots and titanium oxide nanopowders were uniformly dispersed within the chitosan matrix, forming a homogeneous, non-agglomerated coating. The antibacterial assay results confirmed the antibacterial activity of synthesized samples against most of the bacterial pathogens tested but exhibited stronger antibacterial effects on Gram-negative bacteria compared to Gram-positive bacteria. The largest inhibition zones, measuring 21 mm and 16 mm, were observed for Pseudomonas and E. coli for titanium dioxide nanoparticles and the final nanocomposite, respectively. Additionally, the MIC and MBC values for all 7 bacteria were determined. The CS/TiO2 NPs/CQDs bionanocomposite demonstrates advantageous structural and antibacterial properties, positioning it as a highly effective antibacterial agent.

Omid Farokhnasab: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Ayoub Moghadam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Zahra Eslamifar: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Ali Heidary Moghadam: Visualization, Software, Methodology.
